coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor for the detections of very weak acids assisted by auxiliary reagents that contribute to the release of protons
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Weijian
Li, Chun
Sun, Xiaobo
Pan, Wei
Wang, Jinping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor was fabricated by conjugating a pH-sensitive fluorescein, fluoresceinisothiocyanate (FITC), onto the surface of carbon dots (CD-FITCs). The fabrication condition was optimized so as to make the pH sensor possess a typical character of ratiometic probe that the two emission intensities ratio is independent on its concentration. As a result, the CD-FITCs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity to pH, and the as-proposed method for pH sensing is of considerable repeatability. However, the CD-FITCs sensor is still not an effective tool for the direct detections of very weak acids based on pH sensing because of their extremely low dissociated constants. Interestingly, with the help of the auxiliary reagents that contribute to the release of protons, the sensitivities of the CD-FITCs sensor to the very weak acids can be considerably enhanced. For example, the very weak acids, boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride, which can be transformed into relatively strong acids assisted by D-fructose and acetone, was successfully measured using the carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor in the real water samples (tap water and lake water). Moreover, the proposed method can be extended to sensing any analytes in chemical reactions associated with proton release.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor
Very weak acids

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Syntheses of amine-coated CDs and CDs-FITCs
Fluorescence assay of pH in buffer solutions
The interferences from metal ions and ionic strength on the CD-FITCs sensor
Analysis of boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride in the tap water and lake water samples
Synthesis and characterization of CDs and CD-FITCs
FRET-based ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor for the detections of very weak acids assisted by auxiliary reagents

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31572181), Project for Science and Technology Development of Shandong Province (No. 2014GSF121034), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2014CM012).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image of the amine-coated carbon dots; (B) The particle size distribution of CDs; (C) The XRD patterns of CDs and CD-FITCs; (D) FTIR spectra of the CDs, FITC and the CD-FITCs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CD-FITCs (0.06mgmL−1) in 0.1molL−1 phosphate-buffered solutions at various pH values; (B) Sigmoidal calibration curve of the ratio (RI531/I413) versus pH, and the sigmoidal calibration equation is RI531/I413 =8.460 + (0.335-8.460)/(1+e(pH−5.968)/0.645), r2 =0.9996; (C) Linear calibration curve of CD-FITCs presented as a logarithmic plot of (Ri-Rmin)/Rmax-Ri) versus pH, the linear calibration equation is log10 (Ri-Rmin)/Rmax-Ri)=0.659pH-4.036, r=0.998.
The influence of concentration on RI531/I413 of the CD-FITCs prepared in different mass proportions of CDs to FITCs.

(A) The effect of D-fructose on the sensitivity for the detection of boric acid using the CD-FITCs sensor in aqueous sol ution; (B) The effect of acetone on the sensitivity for the detection of hydroxylamine hydrochloride using the CD-FITCs sensor in aqueous solution.

(A) and (C) Fluorescence spectra of the CD-FITCs (0.06mgmL−1) sensor in the lake water and tap water samples containing boric acid at various concentrations in the presence of 0.1molL−1 D-fructose under excitation at 360nm; (B) and (D) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio and the concentration as well as the negative logarithm of the concentration of boric acid in the lake and tap water samples, respectively.

(A) and (B) Fluorescence spectra of the CD-FITCs (0.06mgmL−1) sensor in lake and tap water samples containing hydroxylamine hydrochloride at various concentrations in the presence of 0.001molL−1 acetone under excitation at 360nm; (C) and (D) the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio(RI531/I413) and the negative logarithm of the concentration of hydroxylamine hydrochloride in the lake water and tap water samples, respectively.
Results for detection of Boric acid in spiked Lake water and tap water samples.
Results for detection of Hydroxylamine hydrochloride in spiked Lake water and tap water samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor for the detections of very weak acids assisted by auxiliary reagents that contribute to the release of protons

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao, 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor was fabricated by conjugating a pH-sensitive fluorescein, fluoresceinisothiocyanate (FITC), onto the surface of carbon dots (CD-FITCs). The fabrication condition was optimized so as to make the pH sensor possess a typical character of ratiometic probe that the two emission intensities ratio is independent on its concentration. As a result, the CD-FITCs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity to pH, and the as-proposed method for pH sensing is of considerable repeatability. However, the CD-FITCs sensor is still not an effective tool for the direct detections of very weak acids based on pH sensing because of their extremely low dissociated constants. Interestingly, with the help of the auxiliary reagents that contribute to the release of protons, the sensitivities of the CD-FITCs sensor to the very weak acids can be considerably enhanced. For example, the very weak acids, boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride, which can be transformed into relatively strong acids assisted by D-fructose and acetone, was successfully measured using the carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor in the real water samples (tap water and lake water). Moreover, the proposed method can be extended to sensing any analytes in chemical reactions associated with proton release.

ce:section.ce:para:
Carbon dots (CDs), which are a class of carbon based nanoparticles with size being less than 10nm, have exhibited high performances such as bright fluorescence, biocompatibility, low toxicity, excellent water solubility and environmental friendliness [1–3]. To date, they have been applied in wide areas including bioimaging [1], photodynamic therapy [4], photocatalysis [5], optoelectronic devices [6] and sensors [7,8]. In addition, surface modification can endow carbon dots with more functions, and correspondingly broaden their applications [9]. For example, carbon dots achieved molecular recognition ability for sensing application by means of surface functionalization that materials with specific recognition moiety were covalently conjugated onto the surface of the carbon dots [10]. Among most of the carbon-dot-based sensing approaches, the increasing or decreasing fluorescence intensity is usually defined as the signal responding to the alteration of analyte concentration [11–14]. However, such single wavelength intensity modulation type sensing can be compromised by some other factors, such as the concentration change of sensors and the inhomogeneous distribution, drift of light source or detector, or environmental effects in complex samples [15–19]. In contrast, the ratiometric approaches employing the ratio of two emission intensities at different wavelengths are independent on these factors, and can therefore avoid their interferences [20,21]. Particularly, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), which involves the nonradiative transfer of excitation energy from an excited donor to a proximal ground-state acceptor, has been widely adopted in ratiometric sensing [17]. Generally, in the carbon-dot-based FRET system [22], the carbon dots not only serve as the energy donor, but also as the anchoring site for the organic probe which plays the role as specific recognition element, meanwhile as a response signal generator. For example, organic dyes, naphthalimide-azide and hydroethidine as specific molecules toward H2S and O2
−were covalently coupled onto the surface of carbon dots, to form FRET based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for H2S and O2
− detection with high selectivity and accuracy [10,23]. A pH-sensitive fluorescent dye, fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), covalently linked onto the surface of carbon dots can constitute a FRET based ratiometric pH sensor, in which carbon dots act as donor and FITC as acceptor and recognition moieties for pH [17].

In this work, the conjugation condition of FITC onto the surface of the carbon dots (CD-FITCs) was further optimized so as to make the CD-FITCs possess a typical character of ratiometic sensor that the ratio of the two emission intensities is independent on its concentration. The as-fabricated CD-FITCs conjugate is a promising pH sensor with high sensitivity and selectivity, and the as-proposed method for pH sensing is of considerable repeatability. But it is still not an effective tool for the detections of very weak acids based on pH sensing because of their extremely low dissociated constants. Interestingly, with the help of the auxiliary reagents that contribute to the release of protons, the sensitivity of the CD-FITCs to the very weak acids can be considerably enhanced, so that the very weak acids can be detected based on pH sensing using this carbon-dot-based FRET ratiometric pH sensor. In this article, boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride as examples of very weak acids, which can be transformed into a relatively strong acid assisted by D-fructose and acetone, was successfully measured using the pH sensor in real water samples, such as tap water and lake water.

CaCl2, NiCl2, CoCl2, Fe(NO3)3, FeCl2, MgCl2, Pb(NO3)2, AlCl3, BaCl2, CrCl3, NaCl, NH4HCO3, KCl and Zn(NO3)2 were purchased from Damao Chemical Corp (Tianjin, China). Acetone, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, NH2OHHCl, 1,2-Ethylenediamine and H3BO3 were obtained from Kant Chemical Co., Ltd. (Laiyang, China), D-fructose was supplied by Xinfan Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China), Acrylic acid was received from Guangcheng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All chemicals were used without any further purification. The ultrapure water used throughout all the experiments was purified through Water Purifier Nanopure water system (18.3MΩ.cm).

UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a TU-1901 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Pgeneral, China). Fluorescent measurements were performed with a fluorescence spectrometer F-2500 (Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) of the carbon dots were recovered using a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, German) with Cu-Kα radiation (40kV, 40mA, λ=1.5418Å) at a scanning rate of 1° min−1 in the range from 5° to 80°. The morphology and size were analyzed by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a JEOLJEM-2010 electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200kV. The carbon dots solutions were carefully dripped onto the 300-mesh copper grid coated with a lacy carbon film, and the solvent was evaporated at ambient temperature and pressure. The FTIR spectrum of the carbon dots was measured on a FT-IR200 spectrometer (Thermo, America) with the KBr pellet technique ranging from 400 to 4000cm−1. Elemental analysis was carried out on an Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH variomicro CHNS. Analyses were performed twice and the average values were obtained. The quantum yields (QYs) of the prepared carbon dots were determined using quinine sulfate in 0.1molL−1 H2SO4 (literature QY: 54.6%) as the standard sample by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensities (excitation at 360nm) and absorbance values at 360nm of the carbon dots aqueous solutions with those of quinine sulfate [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses of amine-coated CDs and CDs-FITCs
ce:section.ce:para: The amine-coated CDs and CDs-FITCs were synthesized according to a literature method [17,25], with minor modification. The amine-coated CDs were synthesized by a simple one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis process with acrylic acid and 1, 2-ethanediamine as the carbon source and surface passivation reagent, respectively. Typically, 5.49mL of acrylic acid (80mmol) and 5.35mL of 1, 2-ethanediamine (80mmol) were dissolved in 40mL of water in a 250mL conical flask under vigorous stirring, and then the beaker was placed into a microwave oven and heated for 7min at 720W. After the product was cooled down to room temperature, another 30mL purified water was added and sonicated for 30min. The resultant mixture was centrifuged (10000 r min−1) to remove any large or agglomerated particles, and then the supernatant were dialyzed against purified water using a dialysis membrane with molecular weight cut-off of 500Da for 48h. Finally, the amorphous solid powder of the CDs was collected by lyophilization.

The fabrication condition of CD-FITCs, mass proportion of FITC to CDs, was optimized based on concentration-independence for the two emission intensities ratio. First, the CDs were re-dispersed in 0.1molL−1 NaHCO3 solution at a concentration of 10.0mgmL−1 under mild stirring at room temperature. Then different amounts of FITC were added to the CDs dispersion under N2 atmosphere and stirred for 48h at room temperature in the dark. Then the CD-FITCs solution was purified through dialysis by a cellulose membrane with a molecular weight cut off of approximately 1000Da for 48h. The resultant mixture was then centrifuged (10000 r min−1) to remove any large or agglomerated particles. Finally, the CD-FITCs sensors were dried by lyophilization, and re-dispersed in purified water at a concentration of 6.0mgmL−1. The CD-FITCs solution was stored at 4°C for the following measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of pH in buffer solutions
ce:section.ce:para: A series of standard pH buffers was prepared with 0.1molL−1 NaH2PO4 being adjusted by NaOH or HCl (2.0molL−1) [17,26]. Then, the CD-FITCs stock solution at 6.0mgmL−1 was diluted to 0.06mgmL−1 with buffer solutions at varied pH values, and the resulting solutions were transferred into quartz cells for the fluorescence spectra measurement at λ ex =360nm. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at varied pH values and the fluorescence ratios (RI531/I413) were calculated from the fluorescence intensities at 531 and 413nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interferences from metal ions and ionic strength on the CD-FITCs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Representative metal ions, such as Al3+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, NH4 +, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+ (6.0×10−5 molL−1), were individually added into the phosphate solutions (0.1molL−1, pH=6.0) of the CD-FITCs sensor (0.06mgmL−1). And NaCl at the concentrations of 0.1, 0.5, 1.5, 2.0molL−1 was mixed with CD-FITCs sensor (0.06mgmL−1) in the 0.1molL−1 NaH2PO4 buffer solution (pH=6.0). Then the fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were obtained by the fluorescence spectrophotometer at λ ex =360nm in order to investigate the influences of metal ions and ionic strength on the fluorescence ratios of the CD-FITCs sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride in the tap water and lake water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of the pH sensor for the detections of boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride in artificial systems, lake water samples which obtained from the Hongzi Lake in the campus of Qingdao Agricultural University and tap water samples from our lab were analyzed by the proposed method. The lake water and tap water samples were pretreated by qualitative filter paper and 0.45μm filter membrane to remove the solid suspensions and other impurities [16]. The resultant lake water and the tap water samples were spiked with the different concentrations of boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride. Then the water samples were mixed with the CD-FITCs sensor at the concentration of 0.06mgmL−1 in the presence of 0.1molL−1 D-fructose or 0.001molL−1 acetone. The fluorescence spectra were collected by the fluorescence spectrophotometer under excitation at 360nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs and CD-FITCs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, FITCs were conjugated onto the surface of CDs through the reaction between the amino groups on the surface of CDs and the isothiocyanate groups in FITC, with the purpose of fabricating a carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor (CD-FITCs). The amine-coated CDs were synthesized using a simple one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis process with acrylic acid and 1, 2-ethanediamine as the carbon source and surface passivation agent, respectively [17,25]. Fig. 1 A shows that the prepared CDs have a regular spherical shape and are well dispersed from each other. The particle size was distributed in the range from 1.25 to 3.25nm with an average of 2.25nm (see Fig. 1B), which was obtained by counting the diameter of the dots in the HRTEM image. The morphology of the CD-FITCs observed by HRTEM was not significantly different from that of CDs, indicating that the HRTEM might not distinguish the subtle changes caused by the formation of single molecule layer of FITC on the surfaces of the CDs (data not shown). The XRD patterns were collected to identify the crystalline nature of the CDs and CD-FITCs. As shown in Fig. 1C, a broad peak centered at 2θ=21.3° (d=0.42nm) for CDs and a broad band centered at 23.4° (d=0.38nm) for CD-FITCs were consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of graphitic-like carbon based materials, suggesting that the conjugation of FITCs did not significantly change the crystalline nature of CDs [13]. FTIR was used to characterize the functional groups on the surface of CDs and CD-FITCs (see Fig. 1D). The absorption bands at 3274, 1556 and 1403cm−1 were corresponding to the stretching and bending vibrations of NH, and the stretching vibration of CN, respectively, indicating that the CDs were terminated with rich amino groups on the surface [27,28]. And the disappeared peaks at 3274 and 2053cm−1 (NCS) while the newly-appeared absorption peak at 1203cm−1 (CS) in the spectrum of CD-FITCs suggested that FITCs were conjugated onto the surface of the CDs through the reaction between the amino groups on the surfaces of CDs and the isothiocyanate groups of FITCs [17].

The as-synthesized CDs possess a typical character of carbon dots, a tunable emission, as demonstrated in Fig. S1A. The position of the emission peak was red shifted with excitation wavelength increasing. Correspondingly, the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots increased firstly and then decreased remarkably. Using quinine sulfate (quantum yield 54.6%) as a reference [24], the fluorescent quantum yield of the CDs was measured to be 13.4% under the excitation at 360nm. As demonstrated in Fig. S1B, there is an overlap between the emission of the CDs and the absorption of FITC, which suggests that the CDs and FITC can constitute a donor-acceptor pair of an Fӧrster resonance energy transfer system [17,22], and more efficient energy transfer will result from a greater spectral overlap under higher pH condition. An excitation-dependent emission of the CDs (first emission peak in Fig. S1C) was able to be tuned to match the absorption of FITC, as a result, the emission band of FITC centered at 531nm (second emission peak in Fig. S1C) gradually increased as excitation wavelengths moved from 320 to 420nm. On the other hand, with the pH of the CD-FITCs solution increasing, the emission intensity of FITC moiety at 531nm was enhanced while the emission intensity of CDs at 413nm decreased under excitation at 360nm due to the more efficient energy transfer under higher pH condition (Fig. 2 A). Furthermore, the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 531nm to that at 413nm (RI531/I413) can be plotted as a function of pH, and a sigmoidal curve was well fitted using Boltzmann equation with the calibration equation being RI531/I413 =8.460 + (0.335-8.460)/(1+e(pH−5.968)/0.645), r2 =0.9996(see Fig. 2B). The sigmoid fit to the curve indicates a pKa of 5.968 for the CD-FITCs sensor. A logarithmic plot of (Ri-Rmin)/(Rmax-Ri) versus pH was linearly calibrated as log10(Ri-Rmin)/(Rmax-Ri)=0.659pH-4.036 (r=0.998) in the pH range from 3.5 to 9.0(Fig. 2C). Therefore, the CD-FITCs conjugate is a very excellent FRET-based ratiometric pH sensor.

Concentration-independence for the fluorescence intensity ratio is the most important characteristic of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor. In order to obtain the CD-FITCs of a pH ratiometric sensor with such property, the fabrication condition, mass proportion of FITC to CDs, was optimized based on concentration-independence for the two emission intensities ratio [19]. First, the CD-FITC based sensor was fabricated in four mass proportions of FITC to CDs (3:10, 2:10, 1.5:10 and 1:10). Then the fluorescence intensity ratios were measured at various concentrations for each of the CDs-FITCs fabricated under different conditions. As shown in Fig. 3 , RI531/I413 showed a remarkable increase with their concentrations of the CDs-FITCs fabricated in the mass proportions of FITCs to CDs, 3:10 and 2:10, increasing. While the mass proportion of FITCs to CDs decreased to 1:10, RI531/I413 decreased as the concentration of the CDs-FITCs increased. Their concentration, thus, had influence on RI531/I413 as the CDs-FITCs sensors were prepared in the mass proportions of FITCs to CDs, 3:10, 2:10 and 1:10. When the mass proportion of FITCs to CDs was adjusted to 1.5:10, RI531/I413 of CDs-FITCs did not suffer from its concentration fluctuation, and kept nearly constant with its concentration increasing from 0.04 to 0.22mgmL−1. Therefore, the mass proportion of FITCs to CDs of 1.5:10 was selected as the optimal condition for the fabrication of the CDs-FITCs. And 0.06mgmL−1 was chosen as the concentration of the CDs-FITCs fabricated under the optimal condition for the following measurements. Furthermore, the actual functionalization degree of the CDs for the CDs-FITCs sensor prepared under the optimal condition was estimated by calculating the mass ratio of FITC conjugated to the CDs using two methods of spectrophotometry and elemental analysis (calculation details presented in supporting information). The mass ratio of FITC conjugated to the CDs for the CDs-FITCs sensor was estimated to be 0.171:1 by the spectrophotometry, which is identical to the result of 0.178:1 obtained by the elemental analysis.

The repeatability of CD-FITCs as pH sensor was investigated by measuring RI531/I413 in 0.1molL−1 phosphate-buffered solutions at various pH values for three replicates. As shown in Fig. S2A, the CD-FITCs sensor showed an excellent repeatability in a wide pH range. Photostability of the CD-FITCs sensor was tested by measuring RI531/I413 under continuous excitation at 360nm using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 150W Xe lamp. RI531/I413 exhibited negligible changes within 60min (Fig. S2B), suggesting that the CD-FITCs sensor has an excellent photostablility under light irradiation during detection.

The selectivity of CD-FITCs to pH was further investigated by evaluating the influences of metal ions and ionic strength on R I531/I413. Firstly, the effects of 14 kinds of metal cations, including Al3+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, NH4 +, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+, on R I531/I413 of the CD-FITCs in phosphate buffer solution at pH=6.0 were explored. Compared with blank buffer solution, no significant change in RI531/I413 can be observed for the CD-FITCs in the pH6.0 phosphate buffer solution upon the additions of different metal ions, indicating that the interferences from those metals on RI531/I413 of the CD-FITCs pH sensor are negligible (see Fig. S3A). The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of the CD-FITCs was also investigated, as shown in Fig. S3B. No obvious change was found in the fluorescence intensity ratio of the CD-FITCs with the NaCl concentration increasing from 0.0 to 0.2molL−1, suggesting that ionic strength has no effect on the fluorescence intensity ratio of the CD-FITCs.

Overall, the CD-FITCs conjugate is a considerably promising pH sensor with high sensitivity, selectivity, repeatability, and especially a typical character of ratiometic sensor that the ratio of the two emission intensities is independent on its concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET-based ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor for the detections of very weak acids assisted by auxiliary reagents
ce:section.ce:para:
Although the CD-FITCs based sensor is highly sensitive to pH, it is of a great challenge for the detections of very weak acids based on pH sensing using this FRET-based ratiometric fluorescent pH sensor. For example, boric acid (H3BO3), does not react in aqueous solution as a Bronsted acid, but instead, it behaves as a Lewis acid, with formation of the tetrahedral B(OH)4 − anion (Eq. (1)):
(1) B(OH)3 +H2O⇌B(OH)4 − +H+

It is considered as a very weak acid, with an acid dissociated constant around pKa =9.2 [29]. Yet, the significant change of pH will not result from its concentration variation. Thus, the CD-FITCs sensor is not an effective tool for the direct detection of boric acid based on pH change through self-dissociation associated with concentration (Fig. 4 A). Interestingly, boric acid can be transformed into a relatively strong acid by the addition of D-fructose, producing mainly anionic boron complexes (Eq. (2)).

In the presence of D-fructose that contributes to the release of protons, the concentration variation of boric acid will cause a significant pH change, which can be sensitively detected by the CD-FITCs based pH sensor and converted to the fluorescence intensity ratio (RI531/I413). Based on sensing pH, a linear relationship can be found between RI531/I413 and the concentration of boric acid. As shown in Fig. 4A, with the help of D-fructose, the pH sensor is much more sensitive for the detection of boric acid than that through self-dissociation without the help of the auxiliary reagent (see Fig. 4A).

Similarly, hydroxylamine hydrochloride (NH2OHHCl) also is a very weak acid. Acetone can react with hydroxylamine hydrochloride to produce propan-2-one oxime and release proton in a 1:1 proportion (Eq. (3)). With the help of acetone, the pH sensor showed much more sensitivity for the detection of NH2OHHCl than that without the help of auxiliary agent based on pH sensing as shown in Fig. 4B.

In order to obtain a wide linear range, D-fructose and acetone should be excessive in the detections of boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride. Thus, the effects of D-fructose and acetone on the fluorescence intensity ratio of the CD-FITCs sensor in the aqueous solution should be investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S3C and D, compared with the blank, RI531/I413 was negligibly changed upon the additions of D-fructose or acetone at various concentrations, suggesting that the unreacted D-fructose or acetone will have no obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity ratio of the CD-FITCs sensor during the detections of boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride.

With the help of the auxiliary agents, D-fructose and acetone, the sensitivities for the detections of boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride were considerably enhanced. Combined with the high selectivity to pH, excellent repeatability, and concentration independence, the present CD-FITCs pH sensor might be directly applied for detecting boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride in real water samples. To evaluate feasibility of the pH sensor for the detections of boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride in artificial systems, lake water samples which obtained from the Hongzi Lake in the campus of Qingdao Agricultural University and tap water samples from our lab were analyzed by the proposed method. The lake water and tap water samples were pretreated by qualitative filter paper and 0.45μm filter membrane to remove the solid suspensions and other impurities. The resultant lake water and the tap water samples were spiked with the different concentrations of boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride. Fig. 5 A and C present the fluorescence spectra of the CD-FITCs sensor (0.06mgmL−1) in the lake water and tap water samples, respectively, containing 0.1molL−1 D-fructose and spiked with boric acid at various concentrations. In the high concentration range (>0.01molL−1), the emission intensity of carbon dots moieties at 413nm showed an obvious enhancement with the concentration of boric acid increasing under the excitation at 360nm. However, in the low concentration range (<0.01molL−1), the emission intensity at 413nm showed nearly independent on the concentration of boric acid. On the other hand, the emission of FITC at 531nm gradually decreased with boric acid concentration increasing. Therefore, RI413/I531 in high concentration range while RI531/I413 in low concentration range were applied as response signals to the concentrations of boric acid. For the detection of boric acid in the lake water samples, a good linear relationship (r=0.994) between RI413/I531 and the concentration of boric acid was obtained in the high concentration range from 0.01 to 0.06molL−1, with the linear regression equation being RI413/I531 =0.235+6.343c (c is the concentrations of boric acid). In the low concentration range from 1.0×10−3 to 0.02molL−1, RI531/I413 showed an excellent linear correlation with the logarithm of boric acid concentration (r=0.999), and the linear regression equation is RI531/I413 =−2.438 −2.980log10(c). Combined the two types of ratio of fluorescence intensity, the linear range of concentration can be extended from 0.001 to 0.06molL−1 (Fig. 5B).

Similarly, for tap water samples, there is a good linear relationship (r=0.994) between ratio (RI413/I531) and the logarithm of boric acid concentration in the high concentration range from 0.02 to 0.07molL−1 with a linear regression equation of RI413/I531 =1.371−0.572log10(c). And an excellent linear relationship was calibrated between RI531/I413 and the logarithm of boric acid concentration (RI531/I413 =−5.758 − 4.355log10(c), r=0.999) in the low concentration range from 0.001 to 0.01molL−1 (Fig. 5D). The LOD(3Sd/s) was calculated to be 88.98μmolL−1 for both lake water and tap water samples. The recoveries for the detection of boric acid in lake water samples and tap water samples ranged from 92.5 to 104.2% (see Table 1 ).

For the detections of hydroxylamine hydrochloride in the lake water and tap water samples, good linear relationships were both observed between RI531/I413 and the logarithm of concentration in the range from 1.0 to 10.0mmolL−1 with the regression functions being RI531/I413 =−5.758 − 4.355log10(c) (r=0.999) and RI531/I413 =−5.432 − 4.266log10(c) (r=0.999)(Fig. 6 ), meanwhile, the detection limits were calculated to be 129.494 and 118.122μmolL−1 for lake water and tap water samples, respectively. As presented in Table 2 , the recoveries for the detections of the spiked hydroxylamine hydrochloride in the lake water and tap water samples ranged from 96.5 to 102.5%.
In summary, the CD-FITCs as can be directly applied for detecting boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochloride in the real water samples with high accuracy.

The CD-FITCs conjugate fabricated by covalently linking CDs with fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) is a considerably promising pH sensor with high sensitivity, selectivity, repeatability, and especially a typical character of ratiometic sensor that the ratio of two fluorescence intensities is independent on its concentration. But the CD-FITCs sensor is not an effective tool for the direct detections of very weak acid based on pH change through self-dissociation associated with concentration. However, with the help of the auxiliary reagents that contribute to the release of protons, the sensitivity of the CD-FITCs to the very weak acid can be considerably enhanced. Boric acid and hydroxylamine hydrochlorid as very weak acids can be transformed into relatively strong acids assisted by D-fructose and acetone. As a result, the recoveries ranged from 92.5 to 104.2% for the detections of boric acid, and from 96.5 to 102.5% for hydroxylamine hydrochloride in the lake water samples and tap water samples, respectively. Therefore, this carbon-dot-based pH sensor can be converted to a sensor of very weak acids based on pH sensing with the help of auxiliary agents to release protons. Furthermore, it can be extended to sensing any analytes in reactions associated with proton release.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31572181), Project for Science and Technology Development of Shandong Province (No. 2014GSF121034), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2014CM012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.01.009.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Weijian Liu received her BSc in Material Chemistry from Qingdao Agricultural University in 2015. She is currently a graduate student at Qingdao Agricultural University, majoring in applied chemistry. Her research interests are primarily in preparation of fluorescent probes, and their applications in analytical chemistry.

Chun Li was born in Zhaodong City, Heilongjiang Province, China. He received his BSc from Kunming University of Science and Technology in 2014. He joined Prof. Jinping Wang’s group at Qingdao Agricultural University as a graduate student in 2015. His current research focuses on synthesis of carbon dots and their applications in biosensors and bioimaging.

Xiaobo Sun received his Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2005. Now she is an Associate Professor in College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical sciences at Qingdao Agricultural University, China. Her research interests are primarily in analytical chemistry and separation science.

Wei Pan was born in Shandong, China, in 1979. He received his MSc from Sichuan University in 2006. Then he joined Qingdao Agricultural University in Sep. 2006 as an experimentalist. His research interests focus on functional nanomaterials and devices.

Jinping Wang received his Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2004. He received postdoctoral training at Waseda University in 2007–2009, and worked as a visiting scholar at Clemson University in 2012–2013. Now he is a Full Professor in College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical sciences at Qingdao Agricultural University, China. His research interests are primarily in preparation of carbon-based nanomaterials, and their bioimaging and sensing applications.
